text: The ICRC was founded in 1863 and, arguably, is the leading agency in the world of humanitarian affairs. Over 150 plus years, it has done many wonderful things but a small group of former ICRC officials claim the organization is in a state of decline due to current policies which they claim have harmed the organization’s neutral and independent image as well as undermined its humanitarian competence. Specifically, they claim the organization has grown too rapidly and become too large. It is capable for achieving fewer real humanitarian goals more quickly and efficiently because of the way the organization has developed over the past several decades. In this Issue Brief, David P. Forsythe weighs the pros and cons of ICRC policies and practices over recent years and disagrees with the characterization of a humanitarian agency that has done a tremendous amount of good over time as being in “fatal decline.” There have undoubtedly been some poor staff decisions as well as a few policy mistakes over the years that should be corrected, but an obituary of this humanitarian institution as a whole is premature.
